clips.title: Prelude in C major, BWV 846
clips.reveal: This one is a machine, and it's not even trying to hide it. Every note is exactly the same length and exactly the same volume. A real person always leans into some notes more than others without thinking about it. Once you notice how even it is, it starts to sound like a music box instead of a person.

clips.title: Minuet in G major, BWV Anh. 114
clips.reveal: Machine. A minuet is a dance, and a real player gives it a little bounce. Lighter on beats two and three, a tiny pause at the end of each section, like taking a breath. This version hits every beat with exactly the same weight, so it sounds like someone typing the piece instead of dancing it.

clips.title: Minuet in G major, BWV Anh. 114
clips.reveal: Human. Aaron Dunn gives the dance a real three-step shape: the first beat lands, the next two lift away, and the ends of phrases take a small breath. The machine twin keeps every beat on the same level. Here the pulse has posture, and that comes from a player deciding where the weight belongs.

clips.title: Für Elise, WoO 59 (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine, but a sneakier one. Small random errors were added to the timing and volume so it wouldn't sound perfect. The catch is that real human errors aren't random. A person speeds up going into a phrase and settles coming out of it, so their timing drifts in a direction. This one just wobbles in place.

clips.title: Prelude in A major, Op. 28 No. 7
clips.reveal: Machine. Listen to the chords. All the notes in each chord land at exactly the same instant, every single time. Real pianists almost never do that. They roll big chords a little, let the bass come in early, and let the melody note land last so it sticks out on top. Sixteen bars of perfectly synchronized chords is a computer's handwriting.

clips.title: Gymnopédie No. 1
clips.reveal: Machine, the kind built to fool you. It speeds up and slows down, the melody is louder than the background, and there's even simulated pedal. But it breathes the exact same way every four bars, right on schedule. A real person playing Satie drifts unpredictably. Some bars float and some don't. This one is too regular about being irregular.

clips.title: Prelude in E minor, Op. 28 No. 4
clips.reveal: Machine, playing sad. The swells and slowdowns are programmed in, and honestly they're decent. But every swell peaks in exactly the middle of the phrase and dies down right on cue. Also listen to the repeating left-hand chords. They never waver, even while the melody is supposedly falling apart. A real player's two hands react to each other. These don't.

clips.title: Fantasia in D minor, K. 397 (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine. The rolling arpeggios rise and fall nicely, but every note starts the exact same way, like they were all played by the same finger at the same angle. On a slow arpeggio you can usually hear a real hand moving across the keys, with tiny hesitations when it has to travel. Here, nothing ever has to travel anywhere.

clips.title: Fantasia in D minor, K. 397 (opening)
clips.reveal: Human. Luis Kolodin lets the opening arpeggios search rather than march. The spaces between notes subtly expand as the hand reaches outward, then tighten when it turns back. Each new harmony also arrives with a different shade of weight. That physical travel and changing intention are the clues.

clips.title: Nocturne in E-flat major, Op. 9 No. 2
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. The little ornaments give it away. Those fast decorative turns come out uneven, quicker at the top and relaxing at the bottom, which is just how hands work. Also notice the left hand putting the bass down slightly before the melody answers. That tiny mismatch between the hands is something players do naturally and machines usually don't.

clips.title: Nocturne in E-flat major, Op. 9 No. 2
clips.reveal: Machine, and a fairly persuasive one. Rubato, pedal and melody voicing are all programmed in. The ornaments expose it: every turn is fitted into its allotted space with the same clean geometry, while a pianist compresses and stretches decorations to aim at the next important note.

clips.title: Mazurka in A minor, Op. 17 No. 4
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human, and honestly one of the easier ones. A mazurka doesn't just speed up and slow down. The whole shape of each bar bends, with a lean on the second beat. You can hear the player hesitate at the turns, like they're deciding something in the moment. Software can bend time, but it bends it evenly. This bends like someone dancing.

clips.title: Mazurka in A minor, Op. 17 No. 4
clips.reveal: Machine with small timing and touch variations. It is loose enough to avoid sounding quantized, but the looseness has no dance logic. The second beat never receives the slight lean that gives a mazurka its gait, and phrase endings do not settle; the clock has simply been made a little wobbly.

clips.title: Prelude in C major, Op. 28 No. 1
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human, the kind that fools people. It's fast and almost machine-even. But listen to what happens when it gets louder: it also speeds up, and when it gets quieter it relaxes. Volume and speed moving together is a body thing. It's what excitement sounds like. Random computer jitter can fake sloppiness, but it can't fake wanting to get somewhere.

clips.title: Prelude in C major, Op. 28 No. 1
clips.source: Bernd Krueger, Classical Piano MIDI Page (CC BY-SA Germany)
clips.reveal: Machine. The swell and acceleration are convincing because they were programmed together, but the pattern repeats with identical finger-to-finger clarity even at its most excited. A real hand pays for intensity with tiny changes in balance and spacing. This one gets emotional without ever getting physically involved.

clips.title: Prelude in B minor, Op. 28 No. 6
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. The melody is in the left hand here, low like a cello, and the player weights it unevenly. Some notes swell and some back off. There's also real pedal in a real room. The harmonies blur into each other and get cleared a beat late, when the blur gets too thick. Software changes the pedal exactly on the barline. People change it when their ear says so.

clips.title: Minuet in G minor, BWV Anh. 115
clips.reveal: Machine. It's the minor-key cousin of the famous Minuet in G, played with zero opinion. Real dance music moves in two-bar pairs, a step and an answer. Here every bar has the same weight and the same footfall. Nothing lifts and nothing lands.

clips.title: Minuet in G minor, BWV Anh. 115
clips.reveal: Human. The phrases come in question-and-answer pairs: the first line presses forward and the reply relaxes. Chords do not arrive as perfectly sealed blocks, either; the melody note often speaks a fraction ahead of the inner voices. Those small priorities give the dance a point of view.

clips.title: Kinderszenen: Of Foreign Lands and Peoples
clips.reveal: Machine with random errors sprinkled on top. This piece is supposed to sound like remembering something. Schumann wrote it about faraway lands. The added fuzz makes it less robotic, but the pauses between phrases are all identical and the inner notes never lean toward the melody. Noise isn't the same thing as nostalgia.

clips.title: Waltz in A-flat major, Op. 39 No. 15
clips.reveal: Machine. Waltzes are where machines get caught, because of the lilt. Real players stretch the first beat and lighten the other two. It's a body habit nobody writes down. This version gives all three beats equal weight, and after a couple of bars your ear notices that nobody's dancing.

clips.title: Kinderszenen: Träumerei
clips.reveal: Machine, built to deceive. Traumerei is basically a rubato test, so this render got deep breathing and long, slow arcs. Here's what it can't do: hesitate differently each time. A real player delays that famous climb by a different amount every phrase, like they're not sure the top note will be there. This version is sure. Dreams aren't.

clips.title: Intermezzo in A major, Op. 118 No. 2
clips.reveal: Machine on the hardest setting. The melody is voiced, the tempo bends at the cadences, and that buys a lot of credibility. But listen to the middle of the texture. In human Brahms the inner voices come forward and fall back like people talking over each other. Here the middle stays perfectly polite the whole way through. Brahms is never that polite.

clips.title: Prelude in C major, Op. 11 No. 1
clips.reveal: Machine. At this speed the rippling covers a lot of flaws. The seam is the left hand. Real pianists need time for big jumps, and you can hear a tiny catch before a faraway bass note. This render teleports. No jump ever costs it anything, and hands don't work that way.

clips.title: Prelude in C major, Op. 11 No. 1
clips.reveal: Human—Scriabin himself, preserved on a 1910 reproducing-piano roll. The playback mechanism is mechanical, but the timing and touch were captured from his hands. Phrases surge and recoil asymmetrically, and the bass jumps take real time. It is a century-old recording of human decisions, not software reading notation.

clips.title: Clair de lune (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine, with rubato and simulated pedal, because deadpan Debussy fools nobody. What's missing is the touch. Those opening thirds are supposed to be stroked, with the two notes melting in at very slightly different moments. Here every third arrives as one sealed unit. Pretty, but airless.

clips.title: Clair de lune (opening)
clips.reveal: Human. The quiet opening is held together by touch rather than a grid. Paired notes bloom a little unevenly, the melody waits over changing harmonies, and the pedal is cleared by ear instead of on a fixed boundary. The silence between gestures feels chosen, not measured.

clips.title: Arabesque No. 1 (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine. The giveaway is the two-against-three rhythm. When real hands play two notes against three, the lineup is worked out live and comes out slightly different every bar. That friction is the charm of the piece. The render solves the math perfectly every time, like an arabesque drawn with a ruler.

clips.title: Prelude in G major, Op. 28 No. 3
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human, even though it doesn't sound like it at first. The left hand is a fast, glittery stream that barely wavers, which is very machine-like. But at the phrase turns the stream banks like a bird, easing off as the harmony pivots, and the right hand sits a hair behind the beat, singing over the top. Precision that reacts to the music is human. Precision that ignores it isn't.

clips.title: Prelude in G major, Op. 28 No. 3
clips.source: Bernd Krueger, Classical Piano MIDI Page (CC BY-SA Germany)
clips.reveal: Machine with randomized timing and velocity. The rushing left hand sounds impressively alive, but the right-hand tune never changes how that current flows beneath it. A pianist's hands negotiate with each other at every turn. These two parts remain independent layers.

clips.title: Nocturne in F major, Op. 15 No. 1
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. The melody is genuinely sung. Each note grows out of the one before it, connected by weight moving from finger to finger, and machines still don't fake that well. Also notice how the accompaniment gets out of the way when the tune speaks and fills in during the gaps. Both hands belong to the same person, and you can hear them cooperating.

clips.title: Nocturne in F major, Op. 15 No. 1
clips.performer: Rendered from performance data (Salamander Grand samples)
clips.reveal: Machine rendering. Its source timing came from a captured performance, then this site re-rendered and reshaped it, so it is deliberately difficult. Listen past the rubato to the tone: repeated accompaniment notes have the same clean attack and the programmed phrase arcs peak with suspicious regularity.

clips.title: Mazurka in C major, Op. 24 No. 2
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. The opening is deliberately plain, just simple rocking chords a machine could nail. But even here the chords aren't identical. Each one is weighted a little differently, and the dance's lean onto the second beat shows up before you can name it. A body keeps time differently than a clock does.

clips.title: Mazurka in C major, Op. 24 No. 2
clips.source: Tirol's MIDI Works score sequence (CC BY-NC-SA 2.1 Japan)
clips.reveal: Machine with a lightly loosened clock. The chords vary enough to avoid sounding stamped out, but the dance never develops a stable accent on the second or third beat. Random variation changes the surface; a human dancer changes the shape of the bar.

clips.title: Nocturne in C-sharp minor, Op. 27 No. 1
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. The low, dark arpeggios could almost be a synth patch, until the melody enters and everything changes around it. The accompaniment flattens itself to make room, and the pedal clears late, letting each harmony bleed into the next like ink in water. A render changes pedal on the barline. This player changes it when the blur gets to be too much.

clips.title: Nocturne in C-sharp minor, Op. 27 No. 1
clips.source: Bernd Krueger, Classical Piano MIDI Page (CC BY-SA Germany)
clips.reveal: Machine. The dark opening breathes and uses simulated pedal, but every arpeggio is assembled with the same spacing and the blur clears at regular boundaries. A pianist adjusts the pedal continuously to what the room and harmony are doing. This atmosphere is convincing but scheduled.

clips.title: Moonlight Sonata, third movement (opening)
clips.reveal: Human. This is Paul Pitman's public-domain recording. Strangely, fast furious playing is a good place to hear humanity. The rocketing arpeggios aren't perfectly even (nobody's are, live), and the big chords at the top land a split second late because the hand just traveled two octaves to get there. You can hear risk. A render never almost-misses.

clips.title: Moonlight Sonata, third movement (opening)
clips.source: Bernd Krueger, Classical Piano MIDI Page (CC BY-SA Germany)
clips.reveal: Machine. Small deviations keep the arpeggios from being perfectly quantized, but the huge leaps cost no time and every arrival is equally secure. At this speed a real hand leaves evidence of travel—late chords, squeezed notes, a moment of recovery. This performance never risks anything.

clips.title: Waltz in A-flat major, Op. 69 No. 1 'L'adieu'
clips.reveal: Human. This is Luke Faulkner, recorded for Musopen. The Farewell waltz bends constantly. The opening line lingers, pushes forward, then thinks better of it. Listen to the phrase endings, where the last note often comes early and just fades. That's a singer's habit, breathing at the end of a sentence. Machines spread their rubato out evenly. This player spends it in a few chosen places.

clips.title: Waltz in A-flat major, Op. 69 No. 1 'L'adieu'
clips.source: Tirol's MIDI Works score sequence (CC BY-NC-SA 2.1 Japan)
clips.reveal: Machine with timing and touch variation. The melody bends a little, but every bass note and accompanying chord remain in the same tidy relationship. A pianist separates the waltz's one-two-three layers—bass, lift, lift—and lets the melody float over them. Here everything travels as one package.

clips.title: Prelude in E minor, Op. 28 No. 4
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human, and this same piece shows up in the game as a machine render too, so knowing the tune doesn't help you. The left-hand chords are the tell. They're not even. They lean when the melody leans, thin out when it pulls back, and a couple of times they almost stall before the harmony finally moves. The machine version keeps its cool the whole way through. This one doesn't, on purpose.

clips.title: Prelude in A major, Op. 28 No. 7
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. Sixteen bars of plain chords, which is exactly the texture that exposes machines. Here's the difference: almost every chord is slightly rolled, bass first, melody landing last on top. The dotted rhythms are all a little different from each other, like cousins instead of photocopies. If you've met this prelude's machine twin, its perfectly synchronized chords will never fool you again.

clips.title: Prelude in B minor, Op. 28 No. 6
clips.reveal: Machine, the twin of a human recording that's also in this pool. It sings the left-hand melody with programmed voicing and slows down at the cadences, and it's honestly convincing. What it can't fake is the right hand's repeated notes. A human re-weights each one against the melody's swell. These chime with perfect sameness, like a clock ticking behind a singer.

clips.title: Goldberg Variations: Aria
clips.reveal: Human. This is Kimiko Ishizaka, from the Open Goldberg Variations, a recording crowdfunded specifically so it could be given away for free. Ornaments are like handwriting. Every trill here starts at its own speed, tapers unevenly, and lands early or late depending on where the phrase is headed. A machine executes an ornament. A player negotiates with it. The second trill even hesitates before it resolves.

clips.title: Goldberg Variations: Aria
clips.reveal: Machine, despite the shaped phrases and voiced melody. The ornaments are the tell: each one occupies a precise slot and resolves with identical neatness. In Kimiko Ishizaka's human twin, every decoration has a different contour because it responds to the phrase around it. These are instructions being executed.

clips.title: Prelude in D-flat major, Op. 28 No. 15 'Raindrop'
clips.reveal: Machine, and this piece has a human twin in the pool, so stay sharp. The repeated A-flat is the test. It's supposed to fall like rain, and real players make each drop a tiny bit different. These drops are shaped by a formula, with volume that rises and falls in smooth, symmetrical arcs. Real rain isn't symmetrical.

clips.title: Prelude in D-flat major, Op. 28 No. 15 'Raindrop'
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. That repeated note, the raindrop, is the whole game here. It never repeats exactly. Some drops are softer, some hang back a hair, and they follow the melody's mood instead of a pattern. Playing the same note dozens of times without sounding like a machine is weirdly one of the hardest things pianists do. This one pulls it off.

clips.title: Prelude in C minor, Op. 28 No. 20
clips.reveal: Machine, the twin of a human version elsewhere in the pool. Big slow chords sound impressive rendered, but every note inside each chord hits at the same instant with the same balance, chord after chord. A person voices each chord differently, more thumb here, more pinky there, because each one means something different. These are all the same chord wearing different notes.

clips.title: Prelude in C minor, Op. 28 No. 20
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. Each of these big chords is balanced differently. Sometimes the top note shines, sometimes the bass gets heavy, and they don't land perfectly together. There's a tiny roll, bass first. This prelude is basically thirteen bars of chords, and the player treats every single one like its own decision. The machine twin treats them like a stamp.

clips.title: Gymnopédie No. 3
clips.reveal: Machine. Same trick as the first Gymnopedie: programmed rubato, a floating melody, simulated pedal. And the same flaw. It drifts on a schedule. Every four bars it takes the same breath, the same size. A person spacing out at the piano (which is the correct way to play Satie) doesn't space out this consistently.

clips.title: Gymnopédie No. 3
clips.reveal: Human. Kevin MacLeod lets the melody hover independently over the steady bass-and-chord pattern. The gaps are not repeated on a four-bar schedule: one phrase waits longer, another moves on early, and the pedal blur is cleared when the harmony needs it. The drift has memory rather than randomness.

clips.title: Pathétique Sonata, second movement (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine, taking on one of the most famous slow melodies ever written. The tune sits above the accompaniment and the tempo leans a little, which fools plenty of people. But try singing along and you'll feel it. The melody never stretches its arrival notes. Real players land the big notes late and make up the time afterward. This one just arrives on schedule, every time.

clips.title: Album for the Young: Morning Prayer
clips.reveal: Machine with jitter. This is a little chorale from Tchaikovsky's Album for the Young, and hymn-style chords are hard for renders because every chord needs its own balance. The randomness adds looseness but no intention. All four voices stay equally loud the whole way through, and no real pianist plays a chorale without favoring the top line.

clips.title: Album for the Young: Morning Prayer
clips.reveal: Human. Luis Kolodin balances this little chorale from the top down: the melody sings, inner notes stay soft, and bass notes arrive with enough weight to support without dominating. Chords also open by tiny amounts instead of striking as rigid blocks. Four voices are being guided by one pair of hands.

clips.title: Album for the Young: Old French Song
clips.reveal: Machine. A folk-song melody over a simple accompaniment. The jitter roughs up the surface a bit, but listen to the ends of the phrases. A person telling this little story would let the ends of lines settle, even slightly. These lines all end at full walking speed, like someone reading out loud without punctuation.

clips.title: Album for the Young: Old French Song
clips.reveal: Human. The tune is treated like a remembered song: Luis Kolodin leans into its high points, lets the ends of lines settle, and keeps the accompaniment slightly behind the melody. The machine twin adds random looseness, but it never decides which word in the musical sentence matters.

clips.title: Étude in A-flat major, Op. 25 No. 1 'Aeolian Harp'
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. This etude is a blur of fast notes with a melody hiding on top, and pulling that melody out of the blur is pure hand skill. The little finger plays its note a touch stronger and longer than the thirty-odd notes swirling underneath it. The swells also push and pull the tempo with them. Blur plus intention equals human.

clips.title: Berceuse in D-flat major, Op. 57
clips.performer: Musopen Complete Chopin Collection (public domain, CC0)
clips.reveal: Human. The left hand repeats the same rocking pattern for the entire piece, which sounds like machine territory. But it breathes. The rocking widens a little when the right hand gets fancy and settles when things calm down. Meanwhile the right hand's decorations spill out unevenly, like they're being invented on the spot. A lullaby played by a clock wouldn't put anyone to sleep.

clips.title: Berceuse in D-flat major, Op. 57
clips.performer: Rendered from performance data (Salamander Grand samples)
clips.reveal: Machine rendering built from captured performance data, which makes this a hard one. The timing breathes, but the repeated rocking bass keeps exactly the same polished attack while the programmed expression comes in broad, regular waves. Human timing survived the capture; human tone did not.

clips.title: Prelude in C major, BWV 846
clips.performer: Kimiko Ishizaka (Open Well-Tempered Clavier, public domain)
clips.reveal: Human. This is Kimiko Ishizaka, from a crowdfunded recording released straight into the public domain. This piece is just one broken chord after another, which is why the machine version of it sounds like a music box. Here, every wave of the pattern is shaped a little differently. She leans into the harmonies as they change and eases off as they settle. Same notes, completely different weather.

clips.title: Kinderszenen: Of Foreign Lands and Peoples
clips.reveal: Human. Compare it with the machine version if you meet it. The giveaway is the space between phrases. This player breathes there, sometimes a short breath, sometimes a longer one, and the inner triplets lean gently toward the melody they're carrying. The render pauses the exact same amount every time, like a slideshow on a timer.

clips.title: Kinderszenen: Träumerei
clips.reveal: Human. Traumerei has one famous climb, over and over, and this player treats each one differently. They hesitate a beat longer the second time and arrive a little early the third, like re-telling the same dream and changing details. The machine twin of this piece breathes deeply too, but always by the same amount. Certainty is the tell. This performance isn't certain.

clips.title: Gymnopédie No. 1
clips.reveal: Human. This is Robin Alciatore's public-domain recording. The bass-and-chord sway sounds simple enough for a machine, and the render of this piece fools plenty of people. Listen to the drift. Some bars float longer than others, with no pattern to it, and the melody wanders in slightly behind the accompaniment like someone lost in thought. The machine drifts on a schedule. People don't.

clips.title: Intermezzo in A major, Op. 118 No. 2
clips.reveal: Human. The top melody sings, sure, but the real evidence is in the middle of the texture, where inner voices keep surfacing for a moment and sinking back, like the player suddenly cared about one alto note and then let it go. The machine twin voices the top line politely and leaves the middle alone. Brahms without opinionated inner voices isn't really Brahms.

clips.title: Pathétique Sonata, second movement (opening)
clips.reveal: Human. Sing along with the tune and you'll feel it. The big arrival notes land slightly late, and the time gets paid back in the notes after. That stretch-and-recover is what people mean by phrasing, and it's the exact thing the machine version of this movement can't do. It hits every arrival dead on schedule. Also notice the accompaniment ducking under the melody. That balance is a live decision, sixteen times a bar.

clips.title: Arabesque No. 1 (opening)
clips.performer: Patrizia Prati, live at the Museum of Romanticism, Madrid (CC BY-SA 4.0)
clips.reveal: Human. This is a live performance by Patrizia Prati, room sound and all. The two-against-three rhythm is the test in this piece. Her hands work it out slightly differently every bar, which gives the triplets that unsettled shimmer. The machine version solves the same math perfectly every time. Perfect is exactly what this piece shouldn't be.

clips.title: Für Elise, WoO 59 (opening)
clips.reveal: Human. This is a piano teacher's own recording. You can hear a person deciding things. The famous opening figure eases in rather than starting cold, the left-hand arpeggios roll instead of clicking into place, and the phrase ends settle before the next one starts. The machine version of this piece wobbles randomly instead. Wobble isn't the same as intention, and now you can compare the two directly.

clips.title: Waltz in A-flat major, Op. 39 No. 15
clips.reveal: Human. It's all in the lilt. The first beat of each bar gets stretched and the other two stay light, which is how a waltz breathes when an actual person plays one. Nobody writes that down. Hands just know. The machine twin of this waltz gives all three beats equal weight, and once you've heard the two versions side by side, the difference is hard to miss.

clips.title: Étude in A-flat major, Op. 25 No. 1 'Aeolian Harp'
clips.reveal: Machine, and this piece has a human twin in the pool. The whole étude is a soft blur of fast notes with a melody floating on top. The render brings out the melody with programmed volume, but the blur underneath is perfectly even, like a fountain on a timer. When a person plays it, the blur has weather in it. Little surges and pull-backs that follow the tune. This one just runs.

clips.title: Fantaisie-Impromptu, Op. 66 (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine. This piece is famous for being hard: the right hand plays four notes for every three in the left, at high speed. Real hands negotiate that constantly and land it slightly differently every bar. The render locks the two hands together like gears. It sounds impressive and clean, and that clean is exactly what's wrong with it.

clips.title: Étude in C minor, Op. 10 No. 12 'Revolutionary'
clips.reveal: Machine. The left hand is a nonstop torrent, and a torrent is easy to render because it never gets tired. Listen closely though: every wave of the run has the same shape and the same peak. A real player shapes those runs unevenly, surging where the harmony gets angry and easing where it turns. Also the big right-hand chords land perfectly square, with no roll and no rage.

clips.title: Étude in F minor, Op. 10 No. 9
clips.reveal: Machine. The left hand rocks the same stretched figure the entire time, and the render plays it with perfect stamina. That's actually the clue. This étude exists because that left-hand stretch is uncomfortable, and you can usually hear a human hand managing the discomfort. Little unevennesses that come and go. Here there's no hand, so there's no struggle, and no struggle is the wrong sound for this piece.

clips.title: Pathétique Sonata, third movement (opening)
clips.reveal: Machine. The rondo theme skips along nicely, and speed hides a lot. What it can't hide is the phrase endings. This movement's tune keeps circling back home, and a player lands those returns with a tiny lift, like a period at the end of a sentence. The render runs through them at full speed, so the whole thing feels like one long run-on sentence.

clips.title: Fantaisie-Impromptu, Op. 66 (opening)
clips.reveal: Human. Four notes against three, at speed, worked out live. The friction between the hands comes out slightly different every bar, which gives the surface its shimmer. You can also hear the effort curve: the opening surge presses forward, then the player eases a touch to keep control before the next push. The machine twin has no effort to manage. That's the difference.

clips.title: Étude in C minor, Op. 10 No. 12 'Revolutionary'
clips.reveal: Human. The left-hand runs surge and relax with the harmony instead of repeating one shape, and the big chords up top land with a little grab, slightly rolled, slightly late, the way an angry hand actually hits a piano. This étude is supposed to sound like fury barely under control. Barely under control is not something a render does.

clips.title: Étude in F minor, Op. 10 No. 9
clips.reveal: Human. That left-hand figure is a genuine stretch for a real hand, and you can hear the hand managing it. Some repetitions are a touch wider or later than others, and the discomfort rises and falls with the music. The melody above pushes and pulls against it. The machine twin plays the same notes with perfect stamina, and perfect stamina is exactly the wrong sound here.

clips.title: Pathétique Sonata, third movement (opening)
clips.reveal: Human, from the same public-domain Musopen recording as the slow movement. Listen to how the rondo theme keeps coming home: each return gets a small lift, a lightness on the landing, like the player is glad to see it again. Between returns the runs push forward and pull back with the harmony. The machine twin takes the same corners at exactly the same speed every lap.
